Full prompt

Two heavily loaded adventure touring motorcycles on a South LA sidewalk, precisely parked between a stucco wall and the street. Kickstands down. Heavy-duty U-locks around both front wheels and the wall — they have been left here deliberately, locked in place. KTM helmets on the seats. Pannier cases plastered with overlapping stickers: USHUAIA in capital letters (bottom of South America), flags of Colombia, Bolivia, Chile, Ecuador, Morocco, Central Asian route markers. The tires carry pale desert dust. These bikes have been everywhere. They are not going anywhere now. Between the front wheels on the sidewalk: a glass vigil candle burning in daylight (you can only see the flame by looking for it), a pile of marigolds, a folded note held under a stone. Centered on the stucco wall, at chest height, directly between the two bikes: a large color photograph — printed at 8×10, taped flat with masking tape at the corners — of a man and woman, both in their early fifties, in motorcycle riding gear, helmets tucked under their arms, grinning at the camera on some mountain road. The sky behind them is impossibly blue. They look exactly like people who are exactly where they want to be. The photograph is the largest thing on this section of wall, and the center of everything. Around the photograph: the accumulation of people who have come and left something. Above it, two sets of grey handprints pressed flat in paint — side by side, at shoulder height — the first thing left here. A handwritten letter in blue pen taped below the photograph, the handwriting neat and careful. A strip of paper with 213 area code phone numbers written in marker. A small pencil drawing of two motorcycles. More photographs, smaller, pressed around the edge. Below this, on the sidewalk, the young man sits. Mid-twenties, grey T-shirt, dark circles. He is holding a folded official letter on his knee — not reading it, just holding it. His phone is next to him on the concrete. He has read the letter many times. The street continues: a cyclist going past, a woman not looking, a pickup truck at the curb. The palmtree shadow on the wall does not move. Hyper-photorealistic documentary. The large photograph of the couple is in sharp focus — their faces are legible, joyful, specific. The U-locks gleam dully. The young man's face is in soft focus but his hands and the official letter are sharp.

Full prompt

Two heavily loaded adventure motorcycles parked on a South LA sidewalk, kickstands down, helmets on the seats. Travel-worn pannier cases plastered with stickers from a dozen countries. Windscreens cracked from road grit. Tires carrying red-grey desert dust. The bikes frame the image on both sides. Between the bikes, on the sidewalk: a glass vigil candle, marigolds, a folded note held down by a stone. Rubber-banded to the right handlebar: a large photograph — 8×10 inches, printed on regular paper — showing a man and woman in their fifties in motorcycle gear, helmets under their arms, squinting happily into sun on a mountain road. Their faces are in focus. They look like people who have been happy. On the stucco wall directly behind the gap between the two bikes: two sets of handprints in grey paint, pressed flat, side by side, at shoulder height. The handprints are the largest thing on this section of wall. Around them — crowding in over time — is an accumulation: a handwritten note on a folded sheet, taped under the left handprint. A printed photograph of the same couple at a younger age, taped to the right. A strip of paper with a phone number written in marker. A pressed marigold taped flat. Someone has traced one of the handprints in pen, carefully, around the edge. The young man sits on the sidewalk between the two front wheels, back against the wall beneath the handprints, legs out. He's in his mid-twenties. He's looking at his phone. He has been here for hours. The street continues behind him: a parked truck, someone walking away, a bicycle going past. They do not look. The midday sun erases all shadow. Hyper-photorealistic documentary photography. Medium-format quality — the detail of the stickers on the panniers is fully resolved, the faces in the photograph are legible, the accumulation on the wall reads as lived-in. The handprints and the photograph together are the image's center.

Full prompt

Two motorcycles sit on the South LA sidewalk, precisely parked, kickstands down, helmets resting on the seats. They are heavily loaded, round-the-world tourers: weather-scarred pannier cases, windscreens cracked with fine lines from road grit, tires packed with the red-grey dust of a desert far from here. Stickers on the panniers from countries visited, peeling at the corners. A printed photograph of a smiling couple in riding gear — both 53, squinting into sun, a mountain road behind them — is rubber-banded to one handlebar. Someone has placed a few marigolds and a vigil candle in a glass jar on the sidewalk between the two front wheels. The marigolds are fresh. This was recent. The riders are not here. On the stucco wall behind the motorcycles, at shoulder height, two sets of handprints in grey paint — not graffiti, not a tag, just two pairs of hands pressed flat and then pulled away. The ghost of them. Side by side. They have been there long enough to fade slightly at the edges. On the sidewalk between the motorcycles, a young man in his mid-twenties sits with his back against the wall, legs extended. He is looking at his phone with the particular stillness of someone waiting for a notification that has not come. Dark circles. He has been here a while. He is not crying. He is not not crying. The street continues behind him: a woman pushing a shopping cart, a parked pickup truck with one wheel on the kerb, a child riding a bicycle past without looking. The midday sun erases all shadows. Hyper-photorealistic documentary photography. The motorcycles are in crisp foreground focus — you can read the scuffs on the panniers, the road grit on the tires. The handprints on the wall are the most haunting element. The young man's face is slightly soft in focus but legible. Everything else continues.

vince_20260602_016_s2-06-mural-style

Full prompt

South LA, noon. Corner of 37th Street and a commercial strip. The corner building: HENRY ST. MARKET, flat-roofed stucco. The corner wall: a mural painted directly on the stucco, twelve feet tall — a man's face in thick ochre and umber and burnt sienna, like a Chicano memorial mural, like a Day of the Dead altar face. The eyes are open and white and empty. The paint is fresh: ochre drips run down the stucco and disappear into the riot of flowers below. The entire sidewalk — from the wall to the curb and ten feet in both directions — is a field of marigolds. Orange and yellow, hundreds, thousands of them, overlapping in drifts, spilling into the gutter, climbing the base of the wall. Roses mixed in. Sunflowers. Chrysanthemums. Bundles still in plastic wrap. Glass candles in rows. The vigil overwhelms the street. From the center of this field of flowers, a man in a white T-shirt lies face-down. A security guard in grey uniform is pressed against his back. The man's left arm extends forward through the marigolds, emerging from them at the foreground edge — his open palm, fingers spread, facing upward. A white rose rests just beyond the reach of his fingers. His hand is enormous in the frame, filling the bottom third of the image. The vigil crowd: eight people around the restraint and the flowers. A young woman holds a large framed photograph of a man — his face roughly matching the mural, facing outward. A man in a PSG blue jersey (MBAPPÉ, number 7 on the back) stands with arms at his sides, watching. An older woman in a floral dress has both hands clasped in front of her. A woman in a black hijab stands beside her. Two teenage boys stand apart, not speaking. Wall details: layers of flyers and sun-bleached papers, a laminated face taped at eye height, a burned-down vigil candle on the window ledge. Store window: fruit display, FRUTERÍA, EBT sticker, a small Congolese flag. The rest of the block continues. A woman in blue scrubs is on her phone, back turned. A child holds a grandmother's hand at the curb, watching. Rendered as a Chicano mural style meets documentary photography. Saturated color — the marigolds are gold and deep orange. The mural's white oval eyes face directly forward, directly out of the frame. The hand in the foreground is in sharp, painterly focus.

South LA, noon, corner of 37th Street. HENRY ST. MARKET — flat-roofed stucco, faded cream. The corner wall is covered by a mural: a man's face, enormous, forward-facing, painted in ochre and raw umber. The eyes are blank white ovals. Still wet — ochre paint runs in slow lines down the stucco toward the sidewalk. The ladder stands empty against the wall. The muralist is gone. The mural watches alone. On the sidewalk below: a man in a white T-shirt lies face-down on the pavement. A security guard kneels on his back. The man's left arm extends outward toward the viewer. His hand — open, palm up — fills the entire foreground of the image, close enough that you can see the lines of the palm, the texture of the skin. Just past the reach of his fingers: a single white rose on the concrete. Beyond the hand: the sidewalk is a field of flowers. Marigolds — orange and yellow, hundreds of them, overlapping in drifts up to the curb and against the wall — mixed with roses, carnations, sunflowers, plastic-wrapped bundles still with the rubber bands on. The flowers cover everything. Candles burn in rows along the base of the wall. This vigil has been going on for a long time, and it is also just starting. Amid the flowers and candles, the vigil: a young woman holds a framed portrait of the man, the same face as the mural above her, facing outward toward the viewer. A woman in a black dress kneels at the edge of the flowers, placing more. A man in his fifties stands perfectly still with his hands at his sides. A figure near the back wears a blue football jersey — Paris Saint-Germain — standing very still among the mourners, their back half-turned. A woman at the right edge of the group presses her palms together. The wall behind the vigil is dense: sun-bleached flyers, a laminated photograph of a face at eye height, a glass vigil candle on the window ledge. Store window: FRUTERÍA in red, EBT sticker, ATM sign, small Congolese flag between product displays. The mural's blank white eyes look directly outward, forward. No one in the scene is looking at the mural. Everyone is looking at the man on the ground. Twelve feet away, life continues: a woman in blue scrubs on her phone, back turned. A man loading pallets, never turned around. A child standing with an older woman at the curb, watching from a distance, not approaching. Hyper-photorealistic documentary photograph. Hard noon sun. The face is forward-facing, looking directly outward — at the viewer, at the street, at us. The eyes are not painted. They are blank white ovals. The ochre paint on the cheeks and jaw is still wet — slow rivulets running down the stucco toward the ground. The mural painter stands at the base of the step-ladder, brushes in both hands, looking down at the sidewalk. He has not left. He has not moved. Below the mural, on the sidewalk: a man in a white shirt lies face-down. A security guard kneels on the man's back. The man's left arm is extended outward toward the viewer — his open hand, palm up, occupies the extreme foreground. The hand is the closest thing to the camera. Past the reach of his fingers: a white rose on the concrete. And then more flowers, and more — the entire sidewalk for fifteen feet around the restraint is covered in overlapping flowers: roses in white and red, sunflowers, carnations still in plastic wrap, marigolds piled in drifts at the curb. A young woman stands holding a framed portrait of the same man — the same face again, small this time, at chest height, facing outward. Her face is wet. The man's left arm is extended outward toward the viewer, palm up, open hand filling the foreground. A single white rose has been placed just past the reach of his fingers. On the corner wall of the building — stretching from the roofline down — is a mural in progress: a face, twelve feet tall, in ochre and grey and brown. The face is THE SAME MAN who lies on the sidewalk below. The mural is nearly complete. The jawline, the collar of his white shirt (painted), the strong brow — all finished with care. But the eyes are not yet painted. They are blank white ovals. The mural face looks slightly downward, toward the street level, toward the body below. The muralist is descending the step-ladder, brushes in hand, watching what is happening. He has stopped working. He is coming down. The vigil is already happening around the living restraint. Six white candles burn in a row on the sidewalk — their flames colorless in the noon glare. Roses, a sunflower, carnations laid on the pavement. A young woman holds a framed portrait of the man — the same face again, a third time, in the photograph — at chest height, facing outward. Another woman stands with palms pressed flat together, eyes open this time, watching. A man in a light-blue shirt has both arms at his sides, perfectly still. A glass candle holder with a burned-down stub from a previous vigil sits on the window ledge, wax pooled and hardened around the base. In the store window: FRUTERÍA in red letters, the ATM, an EBT sticker, a small Congolese flag tucked between product displays. The street continues around them. A woman in blue scrubs is on her phone twelve feet away, facing away from the scene. A man continues loading plastic-wrapped pallets onto a flatbed dolly, back to everything. A teenager on a bicycle has stopped at the curb, one foot on the ground, watching from a distance. None of them have come closer. The 37th St street sign is visible on the utility pole above the scene. Hyper-photorealistic documentary photograph. Hard flat noon sun, no shadows softened. The man's left arm is extended outward, palm up. His open hand is in the extreme foreground — slightly out of focus in the way that depth of field works, but its size and position make it the image's gravitational center. The fingers are slightly curled, relaxed. A single white rose has been placed just past the reach of his fingers, on the pavement, by someone who knelt and left. The vigil is happening around and through the restraint, impossibly. Seven white candles in a line on the sidewalk, flames invisible in the noon glare but the wax is real. Roses, a sunflower, a bunch of carnations tied with a rubber band. A teenage girl in a black T-shirt stands holding a large framed photograph of the same man — upright, facing the viewer, his face three times life-size in the frame. Her face is wet. Another woman stands with both palms pressed flat together, looking directly at the prone figure. A man in a short-sleeve shirt stands perfectly still, arms at his sides, watching. Twelve feet to the left — life continues. A heavyset woman in scrubs talks on her phone, facing away from the scene. A teenage boy on a bicycle has stopped at the curb, one foot down, watching from a safe distance. An older man in an LA Dodgers cap continues loading a flatbed dolly with plastic-wrapped pallets, his back to everything. He has not turned around. The stucco wall of HENRY ST. MARKET is dense with accumulated life: layers of paper — flyers for a church service, a missing dog notice, a phone-number-fringe poster (all tabs torn), a laminated photograph of a man's face taped at eye height with the Scotch tape yellowing, a glass candle-holder with a burned-down stub from a previous vigil sitting on the window ledge. On the wall to the left of the sign, a mural is half-complete: a large face, perhaps twelve feet tall, rendered in grey and ochre, eyes not yet painted — just blank white ovals. The muralist has stopped mid-stroke. He stands on a step-ladder at eye height with the half-painted face, brush in hand, looking down at the scene below. He has not descended. He is not painting. Store window: stickers, a hand-lettered FRUTERÍA sign, an ATM machine visible inside, a small Congolese flag tucked between a product display and the glass. Hyper-photorealistic. Documentary photograph quality — grain, the slight lens flare from shooting toward noon sun, the flatness of direct overhead light. The open hand in the foreground is the most tactile, most detailed thing in the image. The framed photograph held by the girl is in perfect focus. The mural's unpainted eyes gaze over everything.

The child's legs hang, feet in small Vans sneakers. The man's right hand is pressed against the back of the child's head. He looks directly into the lens — his expression not panic, not grief, but the specific stillness of someone standing inside an event that has not yet become comprehensible. He is positioned left of centre in the frame. To his right, filling the upper right of the image: the stucco front of a mini-mart. KUWAIT MARKET painted directly on the wall in large sun-faded red letters, fully readable, framing him. Below the sign: dense Arabic text, Los Angeles phone numbers including the 323 area code, handwritten notices, layers of flyers. An EBT/ATM sign in the window. In the doorway: a shirtless man watching. Through the store window: a television showing a news anchor, the screen clearly lit, the broadcast visible. Behind the man and to his left: a crashed MQ-1 Predator drone — grey fuselage, scorched and dented, lying on its side in the lot, one wing pointed upward. It is clearly larger than a car. A crater of shattered asphalt surrounds the nose. Smoke rises from the engine. Behind everything: white noon sky. Two separate columns of dark smoke rising from behind the roofline — one far left, one far right. The LA cityscape at the edges: flat roofs, a utility pole, power lines. A man in a white T-shirt is crouched on the cracked asphalt of a South LA parking lot, one arm around a small child pressed against his chest, both of them staring at something just out of frame to the left. His posture is the universal posture of protection — weight back, body between child and threat, one hand flat on the ground. The child is perhaps five years old, face turned into the man's shoulder. What they are looking at: a crashed MQ-1 Predator drone. The drone's grey fuselage fills the left half of the frame behind them — scorched, one wing sheared off, nose buried in a gouge of fractured asphalt. It is the size of a car. Smoke rises from the engine housing. Debris from the impact — shards of grey composite and asphalt — is scattered between the man and the wreck. Through the store window: the blue rectangle of a television on the counter showing news footage. On the sidewalk in front: a newspaper stand, its top copy showing an aerial photograph. Further back: a woman with a grocery bag stopped on the footpath, hand raised to her face. A teenager filming on a phone. The usual South LA backdrop — flat rooflines, utility poles, a palm tree, power lines against a white-haze sky. In the sky, upper right: a single thin column of dark smoke rising vertically from somewhere beyond the roofline. Its source is off-frame. It is the only thing in the sky. Hyper-photorealistic medium-format documentary photography. Hard noon sun. Shallow depth of field centred on the man and child. The drone's grey fuselage is scorched along one side, one wing crumpled hard against a parked car, the other wing intact and jutting across the frame. The nose has gouged a long skid mark across the asphalt, cracking it in radiating fractures; shards of asphalt and fragments of undercarriage are scattered in a debris field around the nose. Smoke rises steadily from the engine housing. Around it: a loose knot of people, differently affected — a teenage boy stands his ground and films on his phone; a woman with two grocery bags has taken three steps back, one bag dropped on the asphalt, hand raised to her forehead; a man in a white T-shirt is crouched with his child behind a parked car, one hand on the child's back; an older woman in a hijab stands completely still, both hands clasped at her chest, watching; a shirtless man has stopped in the doorway of the store and gripped the frame. Through the mini-mart window: a television on the counter is lit with news footage, the screen a blue rectangle visible past the doorway. Newspapers on the rack outside carry banner photographs of the same drone silhouette. On the building wall: KUWAIT MARKET in large sun-bleached red letters, below it a dense layer of flyers, notices, and spray-painted apartment numbers. The building continues in both directions — stucco, flat roof, utility poles, power lines cutting across the haze. Behind the scene the neighbourhood continues unchanged: parked cars, a laundromat, a palm tree against white sky. Noon sun, direct and brutally hard. Hyper-photorealistic documentary photograph, medium-format: shallow focus on the foreground drone and nearest figures, sharp mid-ground, haze in the distance. Around the drone: a loose semicircle of eight people — a teenager crouched to run one hand along the fuselage, two women with grocery bags stopped mid-stride, a man photographing it on his phone held at arm's length, a child sitting on a man's shoulders straining to see, a woman in a green hijab with her hand over her mouth. A thin line of smoke curls from the engine housing into the white noon sky. On the stucco wall of the mini-mart behind the scene, a hand-painted sign reads KUWAIT MARKET in faded red letters. Bullet-point flyers and faded posters cover the rest of the wall. The concrete is cracked where the drone's nose skidded to a stop. The noon sun is direct and brutal; the drone casts a hard wing-shadow across the asphalt and across the legs of the people standing nearest to it. Utility poles and hanging power lines frame the scene on both sides. The South LA streetscape continues behind — flat roofs, stucco, parked cars — completely unchanged. Hyper-photorealistic digital collage. Medium-format documentary photography aesthetic: shallow depth of field on the drone and the nearest figures, deep focus pulling to haze in the background. missile-horrorvacui-max

2026-06-02: Middle East crisis live: Kuwait reports missile and drone attack; US says it struck Iran radar sites over weekend

vince_20260602_004_missile-horrorvacui-max

Full prompt

The sky above a South LA 7-Eleven is not sky: it is packed with seventeen missiles frozen at different angles and altitudes, six MQ-1 surveillance drones with their slender bodies and pusher propellers hovering at different heights, three enormous rotating radar dishes spinning slowly in mid-air like blossoms, a cargo tanker the length of a city block drifting just above the roofline with patrol boats clustered around it, news helicopters banking in tight spirals, and fighter jets at a dozen different scales. Every power line between the utility poles is strung with old sneakers, yellow emergency tape, wind chimes fashioned from shell casings, foil emergency blankets, and plastic bags that catch the noon light. The exterior walls of the 7-Eleven are papered four layers deep: faded missing-person photographs, newspaper front pages in Arabic and English, a torn ceasefire document with an April date stamp, handwritten notices, printed maps with routes marked in red. On the store roof: six satellite dishes of various sizes, a water tank plastered in stickers, and three people sitting watching the frozen sky. Inside, visible through smudged glass: a man at the register surrounded on all sides by stock stacked to the ceiling — canned goods, bottled water, pallets. On the pavement: food vendors with folding tables loaded with produce, a sleeping child on a bundle of clothing, an elderly man in a plastic chair with his chin on his chest, two women comparing phones, a HUMVEC jammed against the kerb with aid boxes in its bed. The cars on the street are bumper to bumper; every roof and bonnet is covered in pigeons; inside every car a face is pressed against the glass. Every crack in the asphalt has sprouted: dry grass, discarded packaging, broken glass pressed flat. In the foreground crowd: adults carrying overfull bags, children weaving through legs, a folded cot leaned against a utility pole, a pair of crutches. Every window of every building: a face, a plant, an air conditioning unit trailing wire. Every wall: names painted over murals painted over graffiti. The woman loading groceries is ringed by all of it; her noon shadow and the shadow of the largest missile cross on the ground between her feet. South LA: bleached noon concrete, flat-roofed stucco buildings faded to bone-white, utility poles trailing thick black cables, chain-link fences with scraps of cloth caught in the links, cracked sidewalks, empty lots with dry yellow grass, a dense midday haze rising off the asphalt. The geography is specific and legible — a real place baking in the sun. Hyper-photorealistic digital collage. Medium-format sensibility: shallow depth of field, faint lens aberration at the frame edges, visible grain in the deep shadows. ebola-horrorvacui

2026-06-02: WHO calls for community cooperation to contain Ebola outbreak in DRC — protests over protocols for handling victims' bodies in Ituri province

vince_20260602_003_ebola-horrorvacui

Full prompt

At the centre of a South LA street, a body lies under a white sheet, and through it and around it and under the parked cars and up every wall and filling every window box and draping from every power line and piling in every doorway and carpeting every square inch of asphalt and sidewalk from edge to edge of the frame: marigolds, thousands of them, waist-high stems dense as a field, their orange heads so close together they form a continuous surface, yellow finches nesting in their stems, a child's shoe lodged halfway up a stem, empty medicine vials and discarded hazmat gloves caught among the roots, a WHO lanyard hanging from a stem near the body's feet. In the marigold carpet, near the body, candles burn in small glass jars despite the noon sun. The ring of people is not a ring but a multitude filling the frame: on the left side, white Tyvek hazmat suits packed shoulder to shoulder, their visors fogged, the suits dense with handwritten names in black marker, each gloved hand holding an empty syringe upright; on the right, a congregation — elders in full Ituri ceremonial dress with painted faces, mothers carrying infants on their backs, a teenage girl in school uniform with marigold stems woven into her hair, a man in shirtsleeves with a conference lanyard, an older woman holding a framed photograph pressed to her chest. In the crowd: hands holding candles, hands holding photographs, hands holding flowers downward, hands simply open at the sides. Every window of every building is occupied — faces leaning far out, some dropping fistfuls of orange petals that fall in slow arcs through the noon light, some holding candle jars, some simply watching; the sills below every window are thick with flower pots and candle stubs and framed photographs of faces. The walls of both buildings floor-to-eave: painted memorial murals — hundreds of faces, names in multiple scripts, dates, handprints in ochre and terracotta. The sky is invisible: an unbroken field of falling marigold petals fills the upper half of the frame like orange snow, through which shafts of white noon light descend in columns onto the scene below. South LA: bleached noon concrete, flat-roofed stucco buildings faded to bone-white, utility poles trailing thick black cables, chain-link fences with scraps of cloth caught in the links, cracked sidewalks, empty lots with dry yellow grass, a dense midday haze rising off the asphalt. The geography is specific and legible — a real place baking in the sun. Hyper-photorealistic digital collage. castle-horrorvacui

2026-06-02: European leaders condemn Israel's deepening incursion into Lebanon — military captures medieval Beaufort castle, deepest in 26 years

vince_20260602_002_castle-horrorvacui

Full prompt

A Crusader-era castle — Beaufort, grey limestone, thick walls, a gatehouse with a raised portcullis, towers at each corner — has erupted from a South LA residential block, its foundations cracking the asphalt in long radial fractures; the stone walls are dense with centuries of moss and lichen, bird nests packed into every gap in the crenellations, military equipment rope-lashed to the ramparts alongside laundry strung between the towers. On the battlements: soldiers in modern tactical gear stand next to two figures inexplicably in medieval armour, all looking outward, entirely matter-of-fact; beside them, news camera cranes extend over the parapet, satellite uplink trucks parked on the walkways, their cables draping down the outer wall. At the castle gate: UN vehicles bumper-to-bumper, blue-helmeted figures in the crowd below, a news correspondent filming in front of the portcullis with a boom mic extended. The street directly surrounding the castle base is a complete market: crates of oranges and tomatoes stacked against the cracked foundation stone, a phone-case vendor with a rack hanging from a gargoyle, two men playing backgammon on folding chairs, children on bikes threading through adults, a food truck wedged between the castle wall and a bungalow. The bungalow across the street is unchanged — dry lawn, plastic chair, basketball hoop now with a starling nest woven into the rim — but its roof is packed: water tank, three satellite dishes, a family of five watching from deck chairs, caged songbirds in a row. Every house on the block: rooftops occupied, every window a face or a plant or a camera, every wall covered in painted murals, phone numbers, and decades of overlapping notices. Twelve cats at various positions along the castle base, washing themselves. The sky: a formation of jets in tight delta at miniature scale, three news helicopters, a murmuration of starlings banking in a slow spiral above the tallest tower, and a pair of large surveillance balloons drifting at the edges of the frame. Every power line: weighted with shoes, tin cans, prayer flags, the particular density of a neighbourhood that has been here for generations. Scaffolding on one corner of the castle, mid-repair, with orange construction netting. South LA: bleached noon concrete, flat-roofed stucco buildings faded to bone-white, utility poles trailing thick black cables, chain-link fences with scraps of cloth caught in the links, cracked sidewalks, empty lots with dry yellow grass, a dense midday haze rising off the asphalt. The geography is specific and legible — a real place baking in the sun. Hyper-photorealistic digital collage. 2026-06-02: Middle East crisis live: Kuwait reports missile and drone attack; US says it struck Iran radar sites over weekend

vince_20260602_001_missile-horrorvacui

Full prompt

The sky above a South LA 7-Eleven is not sky: it is packed with seventeen missiles frozen at different angles and altitudes, six MQ-1 surveillance drones with their slender bodies and pusher propellers hovering at different heights, three enormous rotating radar dishes spinning slowly in mid-air like blossoms, a cargo tanker the length of a city block drifting just above the roofline with patrol boats clustered around it, news helicopters banking in tight spirals, and fighter jets at a dozen different scales. Every power line between the utility poles is strung with old sneakers, yellow emergency tape, wind chimes fashioned from shell casings, foil emergency blankets, and plastic bags that catch the noon light. The exterior walls of the 7-Eleven are papered four layers deep: faded missing-person photographs, newspaper front pages in Arabic and English, a torn ceasefire document with an April date stamp, handwritten notices, printed maps with routes marked in red. On the store roof: six satellite dishes of various sizes, a water tank plastered in stickers, and three people sitting watching the frozen sky. Inside, visible through smudged glass: a man at the register surrounded on all sides by stock stacked to the ceiling — canned goods, bottled water, pallets. On the pavement: food vendors with folding tables loaded with produce, a sleeping child on a bundle of clothing, an elderly man in a plastic chair with his chin on his chest, two women comparing phones, a HUMVEC jammed against the kerb with aid boxes in its bed. The cars on the street are bumper to bumper; every roof and bonnet is covered in pigeons; inside every car a face is pressed against the glass. Every crack in the asphalt has sprouted: dry grass, discarded packaging, broken glass pressed flat. In the foreground crowd: adults carrying overfull bags, children weaving through legs, a folded cot leaned against a utility pole, a pair of crutches. Every window of every building: a face, a plant, an air conditioning unit trailing wire. Every wall: names painted over murals painted over graffiti. The woman loading groceries is ringed by all of it; her noon shadow and the shadow of the largest missile cross on the ground between her feet. vince_20260601_006_ebola-marigolds-sheet

Full prompt

In the middle of a wide South LA street, a body lies under a white sheet on the asphalt, and through and around it an impossible abundance of marigolds has erupted — dense, waist-high, bright orange flowers on thick green stems growing directly out of the concrete around and beneath the white cloth. People stand in a loose ring at a distance: some in white Tyvek hazmat suits with visors down, some in ordinary clothes, a woman in a green headscarf, a teenage boy in a basketball jersey. The ring is both vigil and cordon. The noon light falls equally on everyone. The stucco buildings on both sides of the street are closed, blinds drawn, but every window has one face watching from behind the glass. vince_20260601_004_missile-frozen-7eleven

Full prompt

A missile the length of a city bus is frozen in mid-fall through the white noon sky directly above a South LA 7-Eleven, its nose tilted at forty-five degrees toward the asphalt, close enough to count the rivets along its grey hull. A bright white vapor trail spirals behind it and dissolves into the sky. On the asphalt below, a woman in a yellow tank top is loading plastic bags into the trunk of a parked car, her back turned to the sky. The missile casts a long shadow that crosses her short noon shadow on the hot ground. Through the store's glass door, slightly ajar, a man behind the register is counting bills. Nobody is looking up.
